wiki.summary: "No Role Modelz" is a song by rapper J. Cole, it released on August 4, 2015 as the third single from his third album, 2014 Forest Hills Drive. The song was produced by Phonix Beats with additional production from Cole, and interpolates Project Pat's "Don't Save Her". Despite stalling at #36 on the Billboard Hot 100 on the week of January 2, 2016, No Role Modelz is currently certified 14× Platinum by the RIAA, comfortably placing it under eligibility of Diamond status. Read more on Last.fm.
wiki.content: "No Role Modelz" is a song by rapper J. Cole, it released on August 4, 2015 as the third single from his third album, 2014 Forest Hills Drive. The song was produced by Phonix Beats with additional production from Cole, and interpolates Project Pat's "Don't Save Her". Despite stalling at #36 on the Billboard Hot 100 on the week of January 2, 2016, No Role Modelz is currently certified 14× Platinum by the RIAA, comfortably placing it under eligibility of Diamond status. On the track, J. Cole believes that shallow, materialistic "L.A. hoes" got that way because their only role models were talentless reality TV stars. But Cole also had no role models growing up, so what does that make him? A conflicted man who indulges in empty lust even though he knows it doesn’t make him happy. No Role Modelz was already a fan favorite on Cole's tour before being released as a single. The "fuck Hollywood" theme of the album, which traces his own rise and response to fame, is particularly clear in this song's lyrics. A clip of a speech by President George W. Bush is featured in an interlude before the song's bridge. No Role Modelz marked Cole's fourth top 40 hit as a lead artist & the highest-charting song from 2014 Forest Hills Drive. On May 20, 2016, the song was officially certified Platinum by the RIAA, making it the first song to do so without a music video. No Role Modelz received mixed reviews from music critics. Craig Jenkins of Pitchfork wrote that No Role Modelz parlays a suspicion about a hook-up being a gold-digger into a tirade about reality TV women lacking respectable public figures crudely suggesting that "she's shallow but the pussy deep." Jenkins wrote that 2014 Forest Hills Drive often plays at a depth it never delivers". Billboard called the song "one of the decade’s great Rap Internet tropes", adding to their list of the 100 songs that defined the 2010's. Read more on Last.fm.
